footnote_definitions.1: openpilot Longitudinal Control (Alpha) is available behind a toggle; the toggle is only available in non-release branches such as `devel` or `master-ci`.
footnote_definitions.2: By default, this car will use the stock Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) for longitudinal control. If the Driver Support Unit (DSU) is disconnected, openpilot ACC will replace stock ACC. NOTE: disconnecting the DSU disables Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB).
footnote_definitions.3: Refers only to the Focus Mk4 (C519) available in Europe/China/Taiwan/Australasia, not the Focus Mk3 (C346) in North and South America/Southeast Asia.
footnote_definitions.4: 2019 Honda Civic 1.6L Diesel Sedan does not have ALC below 12mph.
footnote_definitions.5: Requires a CAN FD panda kit if not using comma 3X for this CAN FD car.
footnote_definitions.6: In the non-US market, openpilot requires the car to come equipped with EyeSight with Lane Keep Assistance.
footnote_definitions.7: Enabling longitudinal control (alpha) will disable all EyeSight functionality, including AEB, LDW, and RAB.
footnote_definitions.8: openpilot operates above 28mph for Camry 4CYL L, 4CYL LE and 4CYL SE which don't have Full-Speed Range Dynamic Radar Cruise Control.
footnote_definitions.9: Not including the China market Kamiq, which is based on the (currently) unsupported PQ34 platform.
footnote_definitions.10: Refers only to the MQB-based European B8 Passat, not the NMS Passat in the USA/China/Mideast markets.
footnote_definitions.11: Some Škoda vehicles are equipped with heated windshields, which are known to block GPS signal needed for some comma 3X functionality.
footnote_definitions.12: Only available for vehicles using a gateway (J533) harness. At this time, vehicles using a camera harness are limited to using stock ACC.
footnote_definitions.13: Model-years 2022 and beyond may have a combined CAN gateway and BCM, which is supported by openpilot in software, but doesn't yet have a harness available from the comma store.
